name: troubleshooting
context: Debug SSH auth failures, host-key mismatches, config surprises, and unstable tunnels
info: SSH failures are often opaque unless you know which layer to inspect: configuration expansion, key selection, host-key trust, remote policy, or network reachability. This node gives the minimum reliable debug workflow for OpenSSH clients, including verbose logging with `-v` through `-vvv`, config inspection with `ssh -G`, key permission fixes, host-key cleanup, and transfer-specific diagnostics for `scp` and `sftp`. Agents should load this node before weakening security controls or retrying blindly.
body:
# SSH Troubleshooting

Start with the client side. OpenSSH already exposes enough detail to tell whether the failure is config resolution, key loading, host verification, or plain connectivity.

## Minimum Debug Sequence

```bash
ssh -vvv host.example.com
ssh -G host.example.com
scp -v local.txt host.example.com:/tmp/
sftp -vvv host.example.com
```

- `-v`, `-vv`, and `-vvv` raise log detail.
- `ssh -G host` prints the fully evaluated client config and exits.
- `scp -v` and `sftp -vvv` show the same transport details for transfers.

## Common Fixes

```bash
chmod 700 ~/.ssh
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/config
ssh-keygen -R host.example.com
ssh-keygen -F host.example.com
```

Bad file permissions can make OpenSSH ignore keys. A stale `known_hosts` entry causes host-key mismatch errors. Incorrect `IdentityFile`, `IdentitiesOnly`, `ProxyJump`, or `User` settings usually show up immediately in `ssh -G` or verbose output.

## Failure Patterns

- `Permission denied (publickey)` means the server rejected every offered key or the client did not offer the expected one. Check [[ssh/keys]], agent state, and `IdentityFile`.
- `Host key verification failed` means the presented server key is unknown or no longer trusted. Load [[ssh/known-hosts]].
- `Connection refused` means you reached the host but nothing is listening on that port.
- `Operation timed out` usually means routing, firewall, security group, or bastion path issues.
- A background tunnel exiting immediately usually means forwarding failed before authentication completed. Use `ExitOnForwardFailure yes` and run foreground first. See [[ssh/tunnel]].

sections.title: Permission denied (publickey)
sections.body: This message means the server did not accept any key the client offered. Common causes: wrong username, wrong `IdentityFile`, agent not loaded, private key unreadable because of permissions, public key missing from remote `authorized_keys`, or `IdentitiesOnly yes` filtering out the wrong set of keys. Use `ssh -vvv` to see which identities were tried and load [[ssh/keys]] or [[ssh/agent]] as needed.

sections.title: Host-key verification errors
sections.body: `Host key verification failed` and `REMOTE HOST IDENTIFICATION HAS CHANGED` are trust failures, not transport failures. Confirm the correct fingerprint out-of-band, inspect with `ssh-keygen -F host`, remove stale entries with `ssh-keygen -R host`, then reconnect. See [[ssh/known-hosts]].

sections.title: Config and connection debugging
sections.body: Use `ssh -G host` to print the effective config after `Host` and `Match` processing. Check whether `User`, `Port`, `IdentityFile`, `ProxyJump`, `ControlPath`, and `StrictHostKeyChecking` are what you expected. Use `scp -v` or `sftp -vvv` for transfer problems, and run tunnels in the foreground before backgrounding them. See [[ssh/config]] and [[ssh/tunnel]].

links.context: Retry interactive or batch SSH commands after narrowing the failure
Fix ignored or missing client identity keys
Inspect whether the expected key is loaded in the agent
Check evaluated config and host-specific settings
Resolve host-key mismatch and trust failures
Diagnose forwarding setup and backgrounding issues

related.note: Most auth failures are identity selection or permission problems
Host-key failures should be repaired, not bypassed
ssh -G reveals whether config expansion matches intent
